revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:07 (UTC)}} {{神社 | 名称=松江護國神社 | 画像=[[File:Matsue-gokoku-jinja01s4592.jpg|300px]] | 所在地=島根県松江市殿町1-15 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|28|36|N|133|02|58.5|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神=護国の英霊 | 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社 | 本殿=[[神明造]] | 創建=[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]） | 例祭=[[10月23日]] }}
'''松江護國神社'''（まつえごこくじんじゃ）は、[[島根県]][[松江市]]の[[松江城]]址にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。
== 概要 == [[明治維新]]後の国難に殉じた旧[[出雲国]]・[[隠岐国]]出身の戦没者2万2千余柱を祀る。島根県内には他に[[濱田護國神社]]がある。
[[昭和]]10年（[[1935年]]）に島根県招魂社建設奉賛会が組織された。昭和14年（[[1939年]]）3月に松江招魂社として創建・鎮座したが、同年4月、招魂社の制度改革により松江護國神社となった。第二次大戦後は島根神社と称していたが、日本の主権回復後の昭和28年（[[1953年]]）に元の社名に復した。
== 外部リンク == [https://www.matsuegokoku.com/ 松江護國神社公式ホームページ]
{{Commonscat-inline|Matsue-Gokoku-jinja}}

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=2017-8-28}}{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name={{ill|Matsue Gokoku Shrine|ja|松江護國神社}} | image=[[File:Matsue-gokoku-jinja01s4592.jpg|300px]] | location={{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}{{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市}} Tonomachi 1-15 | coordinates={{Coord|35|28|36|N|133|02|58.5|E|scale:30000}} | deity={{ill|The spirits of the war dead|ja|護国の英霊}} | shakaku={{ill|Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs|ja|護国神社#指定護国神社|lt=Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs}}・{{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} | honden={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | established={{ill|Showa|ja|昭和}} 14 ({{ill|1939|ja|1939年}}) | main_festival={{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} }}
'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社[[ja:松江護國神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located on the site of {{ill|Matsue Castle|ja|松江城}} in {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}.
== Overview == The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|隠岐国}} who died in national crises after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社}}.
In {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和}} 10 ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}}), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ({{ill|1939|ja|1939年}}), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ({{ill|1953|ja|1953年}}).
On May 10, Showa 40 ({{ill|1965|ja|1965年}}), {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kojun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=2017-8-28}}{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name={{ill|Matsue Gokoku Shrine|ja|松江護國神社}} | image=[[File:Matsue-gokoku-jinja01s4592.jpg|300px]] | location={{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}{{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市}} Tonomachi 1-15 | coordinates={{Coord|35|28|36|N|133|02|58.5|E|scale:30000}} | deity={{ill|The spirits of the war dead|ja|護国の英霊}} | shakaku={{ill|Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs|ja|護国神社#指定護国神社|lt=Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs}}・{{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} | honden={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | established={{ill|Showa|ja|昭和}} 14 ({{ill|1939|ja|1939年}}) | main_festival={{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} }}
'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社[[ja:松江護國神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located on the site of {{ill|Matsue Castle|ja|松江城}} in {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}.
== Overview == The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|隠岐国}} who died in national crises after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社}}.
In {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和}} 10 ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}}), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ({{ill|1939|ja|1939年}}), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ({{ill|1953|ja|1953年}}).
On May 10, Showa 40 ({{ill|1965|ja|1965年}}), {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kojun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

{{Unreferenced|date=2017-8-28}}{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name={{ill|Matsue Gokoku Shrine|ja|松江護國神社}} | image=[[File:Matsue-gokoku-jinja01s4592.jpg|300px]] | location={{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}{{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市}} Tonomachi 1-15 | coordinates={{Coord|35|28|36|N|133|02|58.5|E|scale:30000}} | deity={{ill|The spirits of the war dead|ja|護国の英霊}} | shakaku={{ill|Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs|ja|護国神社#指定護国神社|lt=Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs}}・{{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} | honden={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} | established={{ill|Showa|ja|昭和}} 14 ({{ill|1939|ja|1939年}}) | main_festival={{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} }}
'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社[[ja:松江護國神社]]) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located on the site of {{ill|Matsue Castle|ja|松江城}} in {{ill|Matsue|ja|松江市}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}.
== Overview == The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲国}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|隠岐国}} who died in national crises after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社}}.
In {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和}} 10 ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}}), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ({{ill|1939|ja|1939年}}), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ({{ill|1953|ja|1953年}}).
On May 10, Showa 40 ({{ill|1965|ja|1965年}}), {{ill|Emperor Showa|ja|昭和天皇}} and {{ill|Empress Kojun|ja|香淳皇后}} visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.matsuegokoku.com Matsue Gokoku Shrine]

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located on the site of [[:en:Matsue Castle|Matsue Castle]] in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]].

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]]. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In [[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 10 ([[:en:1935|1935]]), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ([[:en:1939|1939]]), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ([[:en:1953|1953]]).

On May 10, Showa 40 ([[:en:1965|1965]]), [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kojun]] visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|qq=|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located on the site of [[:en:Matsue Castle|Matsue Castle]] in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]].

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]]. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In [[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 10 ([[:en:1935|1935]]), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ([[:en:1939|1939]]), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ([[:en:1953|1953]]).

On May 10, Showa 40 ([[:en:1965|1965]]), [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kojun]] visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|qq=|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ([[:en:1953|1953]]).

On May 10, Showa 40 ([[:en:1965|1965]]), [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kojun]] visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located on the site of [[:en:Matsue Castle|Matsue Castle]] in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]].

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]]. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In [[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 10 ([[:en:1935|1935]]), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ([[:en:1939|1939]]), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ([[:en:1953|1953]]).

On May 10, Showa 40 ([[:en:1965|1965]]), [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kojun]] visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located on the site of [[:en:Matsue Castle|Matsue Castle]] in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]].

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]]. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In [[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 10 ([[:en:1935|1935]]), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ([[:en:1939|1939]]), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ([[:en:1953|1953]]).

On May 10, Showa 40 ([[:en:1965|1965]]), [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kojun]] visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located on the site of [[:en:Matsue Castle|Matsue Castle]] in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]].

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]]. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In [[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 10 ([[:en:1935|1935]]), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ([[:en:1939|1939]]), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ([[:en:1953|1953]]).

On May 10, Showa 40 ([[:en:1965|1965]]), [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kojun]] visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located on the site of [[:en:Matsue Castle|Matsue Castle]] in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]].

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]]. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In [[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 10 ([[:en:1935|1935]]), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ([[:en:1939|1939]]), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ([[:en:1953|1953]]).

On May 10, Showa 40 ([[:en:1965|1965]]), [[:en:Hirohito|Emperor Showa]] and [[:en:Empress Nagako|Empress Kojun]] visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}}) located on the site of {{ill|Matsue Castle|en|Matsue Castle|WD=Q151776|lt=Matsue Castle}} in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}.

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Showa}} 10 ({{ill|1935|en|1935|WD=Q18658|lt=1935}}), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ({{ill|1939|en|1939|WD=Q18639|lt=1939}}), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ({{ill|1953|en|1953|WD=Q18585|lt=1953}}).

On May 10, Showa 40 ({{ill|1965|en|1965|WD=Q2650|lt=1965}}), {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Showa}} and {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|WD=Q230697|lt=Empress Kojun}} visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}}) located on the site of {{ill|Matsue Castle|en|Matsue Castle|WD=Q151776|lt=Matsue Castle}} in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}.

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Showa}} 10 ({{ill|1935|en|1935|WD=Q18658|lt=1935}}), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ({{ill|1939|en|1939|WD=Q18639|lt=1939}}), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ({{ill|1953|en|1953|WD=Q18585|lt=1953}}).

On May 10, Showa 40 ({{ill|1965|en|1965|WD=Q2650|lt=1965}}), {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Showa}} and {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|WD=Q230697|lt=Empress Kojun}} visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

On May 10, Showa 40 ({{ill|1965|en|1965|WD=Q2650|lt=1965}}), {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Showa}} and {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|WD=Q230697|lt=Empress Kojun}} visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}}) located on the site of {{ill|Matsue Castle|en|Matsue Castle|WD=Q151776|lt=Matsue Castle}} in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}.

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Showa}} 10 ({{ill|1935|en|1935|WD=Q18658|lt=1935}}), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ({{ill|1939|en|1939|WD=Q18639|lt=1939}}), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ({{ill|1953|en|1953|WD=Q18585|lt=1953}}).

On May 10, Showa 40 ({{ill|1965|en|1965|WD=Q2650|lt=1965}}), {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Showa}} and {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|WD=Q230697|lt=Empress Kojun}} visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

'''Matsue Gokoku Shrine''' (松江護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}) located on the site of {{ill|Matsue Castle|en|Matsue Castle|WD=Q151776|lt=Matsue Castle|1=Matsue Castle}} in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue|1=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}.

== Overview ==
The shrine enshrines over 22,000 spirits of war dead from the former {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Oki Province|ja|Oki Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} who died in national crises after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}. Another Gokoku Shrine in the prefecture is {{ill|Hamada Gokoku Shrine|ja|濱田護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Gokoku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Hamada Gokoku Shrine|WD=Q11566232}}.

In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Showa|1=Shōwa era}} 10 ({{ill|1935|en|1935|WD=Q18658|lt=1935|1=1935}}), the Shimane Prefecture Shokonsha Construction Support Association was organized. In Showa 14 ({{ill|1939|en|1939|WD=Q18639|lt=1939|1=1939}}), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ({{ill|1953|en|1953|WD=Q18585|lt=1953|1=1953}}).

On May 10, Showa 40 ({{ill|1965|en|1965|WD=Q2650|lt=1965|1=1965}}), {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Emperor Showa|1=Hirohito}} and {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|WD=Q230697|lt=Empress Kojun|1=Empress Nagako}} visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.

In Showa 14 ({{ill|1939|en|1939|qid=Q18639|lt=1939|1=1939}}), it was established and enshrined as Matsue Shokonsha in March, but in April of the same year, due to the reformation of the Shokonsha system, it became Matsue Gokoku Shrine. After {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, it was temporarily called Shimane Shrine, but after Japan's sovereignty was restored, the original name was reinstated in Showa 28 ({{ill|1953|en|1953|qid=Q18585|lt=1953|1=1953}}).

On May 10, Showa 40 ({{ill|1965|en|1965|qid=Q2650|lt=1965|1=1965}}), {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Emperor Showa|1=Hirohito}} and {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|qid=Q230697|lt=Empress Kojun|1=Empress Nagako}} visited the shrine during the 16th {{ill|National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree-Planting Festival|lt=National Tree Planting festival|ja|全国植樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree Planting festival|qq=}}, which was held in the prefecture<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author={{ill|Takeuchi Tadanori|ja|原武史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeuchi Tadanori|1=Takeshi Hara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11409676}} |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=129 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref>.
